text: This is a sixty-eight-year-old male patient with a history of morbid obesity who presented with a symptomatic epigastric ventral hernia. One year prior, following eighty-pound weight loss, the hernia had become symptomatic and painful, preventing him from exercising and interfered with his job as a musician. Given these reasons, the patient was offered a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal ventral hernia repair.

The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon performs an initial inspection of the abdomen. The hernia sac contains omentum and transverse colon, which are carefully reduced using two grasping forceps with gentle downward traction. After complete reduction, the omentum and transverse colon are inspected, showing no signs of ischemia or damage.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum in a linear fashion, performing sharp and blunt dissection to create a preperitoneal plane. Dissection progresses from lateral to medial to address the hernia defect, followed by the reduction of the hernia sac and preperitoneal fat.

The surgeon extends the preperitoneal plane laterally to create sufficient working space. A non-absorbable suture is used to close the fascial defect in a running fashion. The mesh is centrally positioned over the defect with a central tagging suture, and interrupted vicryl sutures are placed circumferentially to anchor the mesh to the fascia. Finally, the peritoneal defect is closed with a running absorbable suture.
Operative time was two hours and a half. Blood loss was minimal. Post-op cost was unremarkable, and the patient was discharged on post-op day one. He was doing well at two-week follow-up.
